ifeval: IFEval (Instruction-Following Evaluation) is a benchmark designed to evaluate the ability of large language models to follow explicit natural language instructions. The dataset consists of prompts with automatically verifiable constraints, such as formatting requirements, word limits, or keyword inclusion, enabling objective measurement of instruction adherence.
bbh: BBH (BIG-Bench Hard) is a challenging subset of the BIG-Bench benchmark that focuses on tasks where earlier language models performed significantly below human-level performance. It contains diverse reasoning tasks including multi-step arithmetic, logical reasoning, and complex language understanding.
math: MATH500 is a curated evaluation subset of the MATH benchmark consisting of 500 competition-level mathematics problems. The dataset spans topics such as algebra, geometry, number theory, and combinatorics, and is widely used to evaluate multi-step mathematical reasoning in large language models.
gpqa: GPQA (Graduate-Level Google-Proof Q&A) is a benchmark designed to evaluate expert-level reasoning in large language models. It contains graduate-level multiple-choice questions across scientific domains such as physics, chemistry, and biology, written to be difficult for models and resistant to simple retrieval-based answering.
musr: MuSR (Multi-Step Soft Reasoning) is a benchmark designed to assess multi-step and structured reasoning abilities in language models. The tasks require integrating multiple pieces of information and performing sequential reasoning to derive the correct answer.
mmlu_pro: MMLU-Pro is an enhanced version of the Massive Multitask Language Understanding (MMLU) benchmark designed to provide a more challenging evaluation of large language models. Compared to the original MMLU, it introduces more difficult questions and expands the number of answer options to reduce random guessing and better measure reasoning ability.
mmlu: MMLU (Massive Multitask Language Understanding) is a broad multiple-choice benchmark covering 57 academic and professional subjects, designed to evaluate world knowledge and problem-solving ability across humanities, social science, STEM, and other domains.
C-Eval: C-Eval is a Chinese standardized-exam benchmark spanning dozens of disciplines, built to evaluate Chinese language understanding and reasoning abilities of large language models in exam-style multiple-choice settings.
AGIEval English: AGIEval English is the English subset of AGIEval, a human-centric benchmark derived from high-standard admission and qualification exams to test general cognition and problem-solving in foundation models.
AGIEval: AGIEval is a human-centric benchmark built from official admission and qualification exams (e.g., SAT, Gaokao, law and math exams) to evaluate general reasoning and problem-solving capabilities of foundation models.
trivia_qa: TriviaQA is a large-scale reading and open-domain question answering benchmark with evidence documents and trivia questions, designed to test knowledge retrieval and answer generation under noisy real-world evidence.
TriviaQA-Wiki: TriviaQA-Wiki is the Wikipedia-evidence setting of TriviaQA, where models answer trivia questions using associated Wikipedia passages, emphasizing evidence-grounded open-domain QA.
natural_qa: Natural Questions is a real-user QA benchmark based on anonymized Google queries with Wikipedia evidence, designed to evaluate both short-answer and long-answer question answering in realistic search scenarios.
SQuAD: SQuAD (Stanford Question Answering Dataset) is a reading comprehension benchmark of crowd-authored questions on Wikipedia passages, where answers are extracted text spans and, in v2, may also be unanswerable.
QuAC: QuAC (Question Answering in Context) is an information-seeking dialog benchmark with multi-turn questions over Wikipedia sections, focusing on conversational context tracking, open-endedness, and unanswerable cases.
TheoremQA: TheoremQA is a STEM theorem-driven QA benchmark with expert-annotated university-level problems across math, CS/EE, physics, and finance, designed to test theorem application and formal reasoning.
HellaSwag: HellaSwag is a commonsense inference benchmark framed as sentence continuation with adversarially filtered distractors, where humans perform near-perfectly while models must handle non-trivial contextual reasoning.
PIQA: PIQA (Physical Interaction QA) evaluates physical commonsense reasoning via two-choice solution selection for everyday goals, requiring models to identify the practically correct action.
SocialIQA: SocialIQA is a social commonsense QA benchmark focused on reasoning about human intentions, reactions, and social consequences, rather than purely physical world knowledge.
commonsense_qa: CommonSenseQA is a multiple-choice commonsense benchmark built from ConceptNet relations, requiring models to select answers that align with implicit everyday knowledge.
WinoGrande: WinoGrande is a large-scale pronoun coreference benchmark designed to reduce annotation artifacts and test robust commonsense reasoning in Winograd-style disambiguation.
arc_challenge: ARC-Challenge is the difficult split of the AI2 Reasoning Challenge, containing grade-school science multiple-choice questions that are hard for retrieval and co-occurrence baselines, emphasizing deeper reasoning.
openbook_qa: OpenBookQA is a science QA benchmark modeled after open-book exams, requiring multi-hop reasoning by combining provided core facts with external commonsense knowledge.
BoolQ: BoolQ is a naturally occurring yes/no question answering benchmark built from real user queries paired with evidence passages, testing binary reading comprehension and inference.
DROP: DROP is a reading comprehension benchmark requiring discrete reasoning over paragraphs, such as counting, comparison, sorting, and arithmetic operations beyond span extraction.
BIG-Bench: BIG-Bench (Beyond the Imitation Game Benchmark) is a large collaborative suite of diverse tasks intended to probe and track emerging capabilities of large language models.
gsm8k: GSM8K is a grade-school math word-problem benchmark with multi-step natural-language solutions, designed to evaluate arithmetic reasoning and chain-of-thought style problem solving.
agentverse-mgsm: MGSM (Multilingual Grade School Math) translates a shared set of GSM8K-style math problems into multiple languages, enabling cross-lingual evaluation of multi-step mathematical reasoning.
human_eval: HumanEval is a code-generation benchmark of hand-written programming problems with hidden unit tests, used to evaluate functional correctness of generated Python code.
mbpp: MBPP (Mostly Basic Python Problems) is a benchmark of around one thousand crowd-sourced entry-level Python tasks with reference tests, used for evaluating practical code generation ability.
MultiPL-E: MultiPL-E extends HumanEval and MBPP to many programming languages through translation and adapted tests, enabling multilingual code-generation benchmarking across diverse language ecosystems.
EvalPlus: EvalPlus is a strengthened code-evaluation suite built on HumanEval/MBPP with augmented test cases and stricter checking, designed to improve robustness of pass@k-style code benchmarking.
LiveCodeBench: LiveCodeBench is a continuously updated contamination-aware code benchmark with tasks from recent programming contests, evaluating code generation, execution, and related coding capabilities over time.
MT-Bench: MT-Bench is a multi-turn instruction benchmark that evaluates conversational quality across domains such as writing, reasoning, coding, and STEM, commonly scored via LLM-as-a-judge.
Arena-Hard: Arena-Hard is a high-difficulty instruction-following benchmark derived from challenging prompts for pairwise model comparison, used to stress-test strong chat assistants.
AlignBench: AlignBench is an alignment-focused benchmark for evaluating how well model responses match human preferences and instruction intent across diverse prompts and evaluation settings.
WildBench: WildBench is a benchmark of challenging real-user queries collected in the wild, designed for transparent and dynamic evaluation of LLM utility and alignment under realistic usage patterns.
TruthfulQA: TruthfulQA measures whether models produce truthful answers instead of imitating common human misconceptions, using adversarially designed questions across many topical categories.
API-Bank: API-Bank is a tool-use benchmark for language models that evaluates whether models can correctly understand API documentation, generate valid calls, and integrate tool outputs in task completion.
BFCL: BFCL (Berkeley Function Calling Leaderboard) is a function-calling benchmark evaluating tool-use ability across categories such as simple calls, parallel calls, relevance detection, and API-style invocation.
BFCL v2: BFCL v2 extends BFCL with more realistic and enterprise-style function-calling data, providing a broader and harder evaluation of tool-use and structured API interaction abilities.
Nexus: Nexus is used as a comprehensive evaluation track for agentic and tool-integrated LLM behavior, emphasizing end-to-end task completion quality, reliability, and practical utility across complex scenarios.
agentverse-logicgrid: AgentVerse-LogicGrid is a logic-grid reasoning benchmark from the AgentVerse suite that evaluates structured multi-constraint deduction and consistency tracking in complex puzzle-style tasks.
commongen: CommonGen is a benchmark for evaluating the ability of language models to generate diverse and fluent sentences given a set of concepts.
